All right, so welcome back. This is going to be Themeplate 7, Slaughterhouse 4. This is a lighter version of the themeplate above that you can use if you're using email to just drive people to a pre-launch asset, whether that's a video, workshop, webinar, challenge, whatever it is. In this case, the email's job isn't to deliver the big shift and a hut itself, but to sell consumption of the asset that does. So you're coaching the click more than that whole belief itself. So here we're going to mimic a shorter formula, the ADA. So this is an attention, interest, desire, and action. The way it's going to break down into Themeplate is we're going to grab that attention right off the bat by calling out that sacred cow and the sour milk it produces. We're going to tease at that aha, but we're not going to deliver it fully. Then we're going to build up the benefits of shifting from that sacred cow to the aha without fully spilling the beans. And I mean, it's weird to say spilling and sour milk in the same container, but we just did it, so we'll roll with it. And then we're going to end by pointing to the video blog, workshop, challenge, webinar, whatever it is to get the full scoop. So an example of this would be, I wrote this in nine minutes, or how long would this take you to write? I'm here to take a stand for you, Ryan. You see, you've been fed this crazy lie that copywriting is this scary esoteric and super hard thing that the average, I just want to get my funnel up and running and make money, entrepreneur, can't handle. And the sheer bolstery of this wouldn't drive me so crazy if it wasn't having such a crippling effect on your business and sanity. So we let off the lie that copywriting is scary, esoteric and super hard and inaccessible to everyday normal online entrepreneurs. And then we get into the sour milk, pushing back your launch dates over and over again as you desperately try to recruit a member of the only profession where it's still cool to drink before noon. Finally, launching, but cringing at your conversion rates and starting to question whether or not people even want your thing. Searching for more quick fixes, those fill in the blank templates that take even more time and effort to shoehorn your message into than it would to do it yourself the right way or worse, investing half your launch budget for a robot or software that supposedly can do it for you. Sorry, selling is still a sentient job, aka your job, as well as a fast, fun and much profitable one once you know your way around a launch email. This is a good size of length, but you're going to want to follow this format. And the reason this one tends to go a little longer is I like to really agitate with the sour milk. I like to spill it all over the place. If you're going to drop and spill some sour milk, you might as well empty the whole container or at least a good portion of it. So that's usually right off the bat. Once you have attention, then you're going to tease the AHA without giving it away. Build up the benefits of shifting from it and make sure you're pointing to the asset that delivers it. So those are the two theme plates for the pre-launch. Just as a reminder, depending on the type of launch you're using, if you're doing a three part video series, you'll be sending three types of these emails to point towards it. You could even write a whole set of these. You can write extra ones to resend to people who didn't click through the first time. So Slaughterhouse four and five are versatile, but they'll work best in that pre-launch. That's what you really want to do to reach up those beliefs, give them those AHAs and insights that are more empowering and move them towards a point where once you launch, the outcome feels inevitable. It just generates all that momentum and really does a heavy lifting of transforming your buyer to someone who can say yes once your car opens. So that's all I have for you here.
